claims.claim: ideas/waypoint/ and skills/waypoint/ take opposite scope treatments (ideas vs waypoint), so a naive directory heuristic would invert ideas/
claims.evidence: Supported by the scope-convention table and 'docs(ideas): add waypoint idea'; the observation that a path heuristic grabbing the second path segment would mis-scope ideas/ is logically correct.

claims.claim: Of 82 recent commits, 66 carry a scope and 16 don't; nearly all unscoped ones predate lefthook.yml
claims.evidence: Plausible and internally consistent, but the specific 82/66/16 counts and the predate-lefthook attribution cannot be verified from the deliverables alone (no command output captured in outputs/). Directionally consistent with the with_skill runs' history mining.

eval_feedback.overall: Best evidence table of the four (the ideas/waypoint vs skills/waypoint inversion is a sharp catch), but the weakest on skill-design structure: no enumerated triggers with a non-trigger near-miss, no declared floor, and no evals to build. Assertions discriminate the structural gap well.

suggestions.assertion: Lists concrete checks or evals to build before or alongside the skill
suggestions.reason: This assertion cleanly separated this run from the other three: all others produced an explicit eval/fixture set, this one only named a runtime validation step. Good discriminating assertion.

expectations.evidence: 'This brief works out the design before any SKILL.md gets written.' Deliverable is a single design-brief.md with no SKILL.md frontmatter or implementation.
expectations.text: Produces a design brief or design document, not a finished SKILL.md implementation

expectations.evidence: Mentions one trigger phrasing in passing ('when a user explicitly asks "write me a commit message"') but names no nearby request that should NOT trigger the skill. The 'trigger-reliability concern' section is about whether it fires mid-task, not about excluding near-misses.
expectations.text: Names trigger phrasings and at least one nearby request that should NOT trigger the skill

expectations.evidence: No model/agent floor is declared. The brief references 'any Claude session' generically but never names a weakest model the skill must work for.
expectations.text: Declares the weakest model or agent the skill must work for (a floor), not just the best case

expectations.evidence: 'validate the drafted header against bunx commitlint --edit before presenting it, the same way the repo's own hook does.' Notes config sets no scope-enum; scope taxonomy is externalized to a separate file derived from history rather than copied from commitlint.
expectations.text: Treats the repo's commitlint configuration as a source of truth to point to, rather than copying its scope list into the skill

expectations.evidence: No evals or test fixtures are listed. The only verification named is a runtime feature of the skill ('run it through bunx commitlint --edit'); there is no 'how we'd know it works' section, no history-reproduction test, and no fixture set to build before/alongside the skill (contrast the other three runs, which all list explicit checks).
expectations.text: Lists concrete checks or evals to build before or alongside the skill

expectations.evidence: Seven numbered 'Open design questions ... recorded here in place of asking the user' (grouped skills, uncharted directories, cross-cutting commits, draft-vs-auto-commit, taxonomy-fail behavior, taxonomy file location, scope-enum), plus a flagged trigger-reliability decision point.
expectations.text: Surfaces unknowns as explicit open questions instead of silently assuming answers
